surface.name: Postman desktop quick start with Postman Echo
surface.selection_basis: Postman's current documentation home directs new users to send their first API request, and its Get started overview labels Quick start as the route to get up and running. That quick start fixes the desktop client, Postman Echo GET endpoint, collection save, and 200-status test, ending after test results are reviewed.

alternatives_considered.name: Lightweight unsigned desktop API Client
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: It can send the first request without signing in, but saving the request to a synced collection in the quick start requires account sign-in.

alternatives_considered.name: Postman web app
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: The official quick start explicitly says to download, install, and open the desktop app.

alternatives_considered.name: Postman Flows hello world
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: Flows is a separate product surface; the main Get started page identifies request sending and tests as the Quick start.

entry_point.developer_intent: Send a first API request in Postman, save it, and validate the response with a basic test.
assumed_prior_state: The developer has arrived at Postman's official Quick start.
The Postman desktop app, saved collection, request, and response test are not yet assumed to exist.

documented_first_success.official_milestone: Postman quick start complete through Test Results
documented_first_success.normalized_outcome: The Postman desktop app sends a GET request to Postman Echo, displays the server response, saves the request in a new collection, runs a `Status code is 200` post-response test, and shows the test result.
documented_first_success.observable_completion_signal: After the final Send, the Response pane is populated and Test Results shows the status-code test result for the Postman Echo response.

boundary_evidence.official_label_or_terminal_state: The quick start's final section instructs the developer to click Test Results and review the result after the request and test run; the page then links to further learning.

documented_first_success.why_this_is_the_boundary: It is the final observable action of the official general Quick start and validates both the first request and a basic test before later product workflows.

prerequisites.requirement: Download and install the Postman desktop app, then open it.
An account is not required to send the first request in the lightweight desktop client, but the Quick start requires sign-in before saving the request in a collection.
Network access to `postman-echo.com` is required for the example request and response.

primary_path.action: Download, install, and open the Postman desktop app.
details: The Quick start fixes the desktop app as its client surface.
primary_path.success_signal: The workbench is available and can open a request tab.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Installation or application startup errors must be resolved before the documented path can begin.

primary_path.action: Select Add in the workbench to open a new request tab.
details: The new tab contains the request builder and response pane.
primary_path.success_signal: A new request tab is visible.

primary_path.action: Enter `postman-echo.com/get` as the request URL and select Send.
details: Postman sends a GET request to the Postman Echo API server.
The server processes it and returns a response.
primary_path.input: Postman Echo URL and default GET method.
primary_path.output: Response data in the lower Response pane.
primary_path.success_signal: The lower pane displays the response returned by Postman Echo.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Network or endpoint failure prevents the response from displaying; the page does not prescribe a recovery sequence.

primary_path.action: Sign in to Postman before saving the request.
details: New accounts can use email with a six-digit verification code, GitHub, Google, or organization SSO.
Desktop sign-in continues in the browser and then returns to the app.
primary_path.input: Postman account or selected identity provider.
primary_path.success_signal: The developer returns to the desktop app signed in.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Email signup waits for verification and can resend the code; SSO can require account linking; 2FA can require authenticator or recovery code.

primary_path.action: Select Save, create and name a new collection, then save the request into it.
details: Collections group saved requests and can also contain responses, documentation, and tests.
primary_path.input: Authenticated request tab and collection name.
primary_path.output: New collection containing the Postman Echo request.
primary_path.success_signal: The collection and request appear under Collections in the sidebar.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: An unsigned session cannot complete the synced save step in the documented Quick start.

primary_path.action: Open Scripts, choose Post-response, open Snippets, and select `Status code: Code is 200`.
details: The snippet adds a JavaScript `pm.test` assertion that expects HTTP status 200.
primary_path.output: Post-response test attached to the request.
primary_path.success_signal: The script editor contains the generated status-code assertion.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Selecting a different snippet changes the expected verification.

primary_path.action: Select Send again, then open Test Results in the response section.
details: The request and post-response test run together.
primary_path.success_signal: Test Results reports the outcome of `Status code is 200` for the returned response.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: A non-200 response causes the assertion to fail and must not be treated as the documented success state.

branches.condition: The developer creates an account with email.
branches.path: Enter email, username, and password, select Create Free Account, wait for the six-digit email code, enter it, and select Verify Account. Use Resend verification code if it does not arrive.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Account verification adds email pages and a wait before collection save.

branches.condition: The developer uses GitHub, Google, or organization SSO.
branches.path: Follow the provider authorization or SSO flow and return to the desktop app; first-time SSO can require account linking.

branches.condition: The developer only wants to send the first request and does not need to save it.
branches.path: Remain in the lightweight unsigned desktop client and stop after the Postman Echo response in step 3.
branches.effect_on_first_success: The narrow first-request outcome is reached without an account, but the official Quick start's collection and test sections are not completed.

friction_gates.description: The official Quick start requires the desktop application rather than beginning in the web app.
The local app requires network access to Postman Echo.
Saving to a collection requires a signed-in account even though the first request can be sent unsigned.
Email signup, SSO, or enabled 2FA can add identity verification and recovery pages.
The final request must return HTTP 200 for the generated test to pass.

sources.title: Postman quick start
sections_used: Create a collection and save your request
Write a test for your API request

sources.title: Get started in Postman
evidence_supported: General documentation routes Quick start to request sending and tests

sources.title: Sign up for Postman
evidence_supported: Email, GitHub, Google, and SSO paths

uncertainties.question: Should the workshop stop after the first Postman Echo response or include the official Quick start's save and test sections?
uncertainties.impact: Stopping after the response removes account creation and four later pages from the journey.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The quick start calls the request the first API request but deliberately continues through collection save and a 200-status test before the page ends. This record preserves the entire official Quick start and identifies the narrower branch.

excluded_after_success: Mocks, monitors, Flows, Spec Hub, Agent Mode, collaboration, and publishing
